runs.interpretation: Medium-run elasticity of long-term capital gains realizations with respect to the capital-gains marginal tax rate for U.S. individuals.
citations: Auerbach & Siegel (2000), 'Capital Gains Realizations of the Rich and Sophisticated', AER Papers & Proceedings
Auten, Burman & Randolph (1989), 'Estimation and Interpretation of Capital Gains Realization Behavior', National Tax Journal
Congressional Budget Office (2010), 'Taxing Capital Income: Effective Rates and Approaches to Reform'
runs.reasoningSummary: The literature on capital gains realizations elasticity generally finds negative values, meaning higher tax rates reduce realizations. Short-run (transitory) elasticities tend to be large and negative (−2 to −3 or more) due to timing/lock-in effects, while long-run elasticities are substantially smaller in magnitude. Medium-run estimates—roughly 2–5 year horizons—typically fall in the range of −0.5 to −1.0, with some studies finding values as low as −1.5 and others closer to −0.3. The Joint Committee on Taxation and CBO have historically used medium-run elasticities around −0.7 for scoring purposes. I center my distribution around −0.7 with meaningful uncertainty in both directions, reflecting genuine disagreement in the empirical literature.

Short-run (transitory) elasticities tend to be large and negative (\u22122 to \u22123 or more) due to timing/lock-in effects, while long-run elasticities are substantially smaller in magnitude. Medium-run estimates\u2014roughly 2\u20135 year horizons\u2014typically fall in the range of \u22120.5 to \u22121.0, with some studies finding values as low as \u22121.5 and others closer to \u22120.3. The Joint Committee on Taxation and CBO have historically used medium-run elasticities around \u22120.7 for scoring purposes. I center my distribution around \u22120.7 with meaningful uncertainty in both directions, reflecting genuine disagreement in the empirical literature."}

- Population/context: Individuals with long-term capital gains in the United States

runs.interpretation: Medium-run elasticity of long-term capital gains realizations with respect to the capital-gains marginal tax rate for U.S. individuals.
citations: Auerbach, A.J. (1988). 'Capital Gains Taxation in the United States.' Brookings Papers on Economic Activity.

Gravelle, J.G. (1994). 'The Economic Effects of Taxing Capital Income.' MIT Press / CRS reports on capital gains elasticity estimates.
runs.reasoningSummary: The literature on capital gains realizations elasticity is wide-ranging. Short-run (timing) elasticities are very large (sometimes -3 to -5) because taxpayers can easily defer or accelerate realizations; medium-run elasticities are considerably smaller in magnitude. The medium-run consensus from the empirical literature (Auerbach, Burman-Randolph, CBO/Treasury analyses) tends to cluster in the range of roughly -0.5 to -1.0, with some studies finding values closer to -0.3 and others near -1.2. I center my median at -0.7, reflecting the moderate negative relationship: a 1% increase in the marginal tax rate reduces realizations by about 0.7% in the medium run. The uncertainty is meaningful because identification strategies differ substantially across studies.

runs.interpretation: Medium-run elasticity of long-term capital gains realizations with respect to the capital-gains marginal tax rate for U.S. individuals.
citations: Auerbach, A.J. (1988). 'Capital Gains Taxation in the United States.' Brookings Papers on Economic Activity.

Congressional Budget Office (2002). 'The Effect of Tax Changes on Capital Gains Realizations.'
runs.reasoningSummary: The literature on capital gains realizations elasticity consistently finds a negative relationship: higher tax rates reduce realizations, largely due to the lock-in effect. Short-run elasticities tend to be large (in magnitude, often -2 to -4) because taxpayers time realizations around anticipated tax changes. Medium-run elasticities are more moderate. The CBO and mainstream tax policy models (e.g., Joint Committee on Taxation) have generally used medium-run elasticities in the range of roughly -0.5 to -1.0. Burman and Randolph (1994) distinguished transitory from permanent elasticities, finding the permanent (medium-run) elasticity considerably smaller than the transitory one. A central estimate around -0.75 reflects the consensus in mainstream policy modeling, with substantial uncertainty—the distribution spans roughly -1.5 to -0.2.

runs.interpretation: Medium-run elasticity of long-term capital gains realizations with respect to the capital-gains marginal tax rate for U.S. individuals.
citations: Auerbach (1988) – reviewed capital gains realizations elasticity estimates, noting wide range roughly -0.5 to -2.0 in the literature
Feldstein, Slemrod, and Yitzhaki (1980) – early empirical estimate of large negative elasticity of realizations with respect to tax rate
Congressional Budget Office (CBO) various budget analyses – typically use a medium-run elasticity around -0.5 to -1.0 for scoring purposes
runs.reasoningSummary: The capital gains realizations elasticity literature spans a wide range. Short-run (timing/lock-in) elasticities can be very large in magnitude (e.g., -2 to -3), while long-run estimates tend to be smaller. Medium-run estimates used in policy scoring (e.g., CBO, JCT) often cluster around -0.5 to -1.0. Seminal empirical studies (Feldstein et al., Auerbach) found estimates that varied considerably with methodology. The sign is unambiguously negative (higher tax rate → fewer realizations). I center my belief at -0.75 as a reasonable medium-run estimate, with substantial uncertainty on both sides reflecting methodological disagreements and sensitivity to the time horizon and estimation approach.

runs.reasoningSummary: The literature on capital gains realizations distinguishes short-run (timing/lock-in) elasticities, which are very large in magnitude (sometimes -2 to -4), from medium-run elasticities that net out pure timing effects. Medium-run estimates commonly cited in tax modeling contexts (e.g., CBO, JCT, Treasury) cluster around -0.5 to -1.0. The Burman-Randolph (1994) panel study found transitory elasticities near -6 but permanent (medium-to-long-run) elasticities closer to -0.5. The Tax Policy Center and CBO typically use values around -0.7 to -0.8 for medium-run policy scoring. I center my median at -0.7, with uncertainty spanning roughly -0.1 to -1.5 across the 5th–95th percentile range, reflecting genuine disagreement in the empirical literature and the sensitivity to methodology (panel vs. aggregate, tax-reform episodes studied).

I center my median at -0.75 with meaningful uncertainty on both sides.

runs.reasoningSummary: The literature on capital gains realizations elasticity distinguishes short-run (lock-in) and long-run effects. Short-run elasticities can be very large in magnitude (around -2 to -6) due to timing responses, while long-run or permanent elasticities are more modest. Medium-run estimates—which capture behavioral responses over several years but not the full long-run portfolio adjustment—tend to cluster around -0.5 to -1.0 in most serious empirical work. The CBO and Treasury have historically used values in the range of -0.5 to -0.9 for medium-run policy analysis. Some studies find smaller magnitudes (around -0.3 to -0.5) and others larger (around -1.0 to -1.5). I center my median at -0.7, reflecting the middle of the credible empirical range, with substantial uncertainty on both sides.

runs.interpretation: Medium-run elasticity of long-term capital gains realizations with respect to the capital-gains marginal tax rate for U.S. individuals.
citations: Auerbach & Siegel (2000), 'Capital Gains Realizations of the Rich and Sophisticated', AEA Papers and Proceedings

runs.reasoningSummary: The capital gains realizations elasticity is negative (higher tax rates reduce realizations, partly via the lock-in effect) and the literature spans a wide range. Short-run elasticities are often estimated very large in magnitude (−3 to −6) due to timing/anticipation effects. Medium-run estimates, which dampen the transitory timing response, tend to cluster in the −0.5 to −1.5 range. The CBO and Treasury have historically used values around −0.7 to −0.8 for medium-run modeling. I center my estimate at −0.75 with substantial uncertainty, acknowledging that some studies find smaller magnitudes (~−0.3 to −0.5) and others find larger ones (~−1.2 to −1.5) depending on specification and data period.

Short-run elasticities are often estimated very large in magnitude (\u22123 to \u22126) due to timing/anticipation effects. Medium-run estimates, which dampen the transitory timing response, tend to cluster in the \u22120.5 to \u22121.5 range. The CBO and Treasury have historically used values around \u22120.7 to \u22120.8 for medium-run modeling. I center my estimate at \u22120.75 with substantial uncertainty, acknowledging that some studies find smaller magnitudes (~\u22120.3 to \u22120.5) and others find larger ones (~\u22121.2 to \u22121.5) depending on specification and data period."}

runs.reasoningSummary: The literature on capital gains realizations distinguishes short-run (lock-in/timing) elasticities, which can be very large in magnitude (−3 to −5 or more), from medium-run elasticities where some timing effects wash out. The medium-run elasticity is generally estimated in the range of roughly −0.5 to −1.5 in most credible studies, with a central tendency around −0.7 to −1.0. The sign is negative because higher tax rates discourage realizations (lock-in effect dominates). There is genuine uncertainty—some studies find smaller magnitudes around −0.3 to −0.5, while others find −1.2 or larger in the medium run. I place the median at −0.7, with substantial spread reflecting methodological disagreement.

runs.interpretation: Medium-run elasticity of long-term capital gains realizations with respect to the capital-gains marginal tax rate for U.S. individuals.
citations: Auerbach (1988) – review of capital gains taxation and realizations behavior, surveying a range of elasticity estimates
Feldstein, Slemrod, and Yitzhaki (1980) – early econometric estimate of capital gains realizations response to tax rates
Congressional Budget Office (various) – CBO typically uses an elasticity around -0.5 to -0.75 for medium-run budget scoring of capital gains
runs.reasoningSummary: The empirical literature on capital gains realizations elasticity spans a wide range. Short-run (lock-in/timing) elasticities tend to be very large in magnitude (e.g., -2 to -4), while long-run or permanent-rate-change elasticities are considerably smaller. The "medium-run" concept—capturing behavioral responses over a few years but not pure transitory timing—is typically estimated in the range of roughly -0.5 to -1.0 in mainstream policy models. The CBO and JCT have historically used values near -0.5 to -0.75 for scoring purposes. Some studies (e.g., Feldstein et al., Auerbach) find larger magnitudes, while more recent panel studies find smaller ones. I center my belief around -0.75 with meaningful uncertainty on both sides, skewed toward larger negative values given the broad empirical spread.

runs.interpretation: Medium-run elasticity of long-term capital gains realizations with respect to the capital-gains marginal tax rate for U.S. individuals.
citations: Auerbach & Siegel (2000), 'Capital Gains Realizations of the Rich and Sophisticated', AEA Papers and Proceedings
Burman & Randolph (1994), 'Measuring Permanent Responses to Capital-Gains Tax Changes in Panel Data', American Economic Review
Congressional Budget Office (various years), capital gains tax revenue estimates using elasticity assumptions around -0.5 to -1.0
runs.reasoningSummary: The capital gains realizations elasticity is expected to be negative—higher tax rates reduce realizations due to the lock-in effect. The literature distinguishes short-run (very large negative, often -3 to -5) from medium-run elasticities, which are substantially smaller in magnitude. Medium-run estimates from panel studies and policy models (e.g., CBO, JCT) typically cluster around -0.5 to -1.0. Burman & Randolph (1994) estimate a permanent elasticity around -0.18 to -0.35, while other studies find somewhat larger medium-run values. The JCT and CBO conventional scoring often uses values around -0.5 to -0.7 for medium-run policy analysis. I center my belief at -0.7 with meaningful uncertainty spanning roughly -0.2 to -1.5.
